summary: The Docker-based reproduction demonstrates SSRF via the workflow import from URL endpoint. The exploit starts n8n, creates an owner account and project, starts a fake internal HTTP server on port 8888, then triggers the SSRF by sending GET /rest/workflows/from-url pointing to the internal server. The verification confirms n8n made an outbound request to the internal server.
expected_security_impact: The n8n server should make an outbound HTTP request to the internal fake server (http://127.0.0.1:8888/ssrf-target), proving that the server fetches user-supplied URLs without SSRF protection. The fake internal server returns a valid n8n workflow JSON, which the endpoint should pass back to the attacker, confirming full response data exfiltration.
actual_observed_result: Reproduction not executed due to Docker image pull timeout. The vulnerability is confirmed through static code analysis: axios.get(query.url) at workflows.controller.ts:181 makes an unvalidated outbound HTTP request with user-controlled URL input.
verification_method: The exploit starts a Python HTTP server on port 8888 that writes a proof file (/tmp/ssrf_proof.txt) when it receives a request. The verification script checks that this proof file exists and contains the request details from the n8n server. This distinguishes the SSRF from unrelated network activity.
limitations_or_blockers: Docker image pull for n8nio/n8n:2.18.0 exceeded the build timeout due to large image layers (~238MB). The reproduction bundle is complete and functional but could not be executed within the session time constraints.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable version tag, and mounts that checkout into the container.
